followup:
Pausing to address the Zoom SDK question first, since it could redirect the spike work.

**Zoom SDK landscape (no Go bindings exist anywhere):**

- **Zoom Apps SDK** (in-meeting side panel): JS only. Runs inside Zoom's webview. JS/TS forced.
- **Zoom Meeting SDK** (native client embed): C++/.NET/iOS/Android/macOS + a web JS SDK. No Go.
- **Zoom Meeting Bot SDK** (Linux headless bot — joins as participant): C++ only. People who want this without writing C++ pay Recall.ai to wrap it.
- **Zoom REST API / webhooks**: HTTP, language-agnostic. Go fine.

Mapped to the three deployment shapes from the design doc:

| Shape | What it needs | Go viable? |
|---|---|---|
| (a) AI is the host (bot joins) | Meeting Bot SDK or Recall.ai | **No bot in Go.** C++ wrapper or Recall.ai service |
| (b) Build outside Zoom (v2) | Whatever WebRTC + UI stack we pick | Yes for backend; UI is browser anyway |
| (c) Audio relay in front of Zoom (v1) | OS audio plumbing (PipeWire/Pulse), control socket | **Yes — pure Go** |

So Go is correct for the v1 wedge and any backend (allocator, policy engine, REST/webhooks). The in-Zoom UI panel (if we ever build one) is JS-mandatory. A participant bot is C++ or Recall.ai — not Go.

Going to capture that as an ADR and proceed with Go for the v1 audio relay + a TS prototype for the bid-heatmap UI. Writing the foundational files now in parallel.

followup: Relay finished too fast (96kB consumed before nc connected, then on exit it removes the socket). Using an infinite stream and killing manually.

args.command:
(cat /dev/zero | ./bin/antiphon-relay -ctl /tmp/antiphon-relay-test.sock -gain 1.0 > /dev/null) &
RELAY_PID=$!
# wait for socket to appear
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do [ -S /tmp/antiphon-relay-test.sock ] && break; sleep 0.1; done
[ -S /tmp/antiphon-relay-test.sock ] || { echo "socket never appeared"; kill $RELAY_PID 2>/dev/null; exit 1; }

for cmd in GET MUTE GET DUCK GET 'GAIN 0.5' GET UNMUTE GET 'GAIN -3' GET BOGUS; do
  reply=$(printf '%s\n' "$cmd" | nc -U -q1 -W1 /tmp/antiphon-relay-test.sock 2>&1)
  printf '[%-10s] -> %s\n' "$cmd" "$reply"
done

kill $RELAY_PID 2>/dev/null
wait $RELAY_PID 2>/dev/null
echo "--- done ---"
args.description: Exercise control socket with persistent stream

Bootstrap complete and committed locally. Summary:

**What's in the repo** (`git log` shows one commit, 40 files)
- `docs/antiphon.md` — the design sketch, moved under docs

followup:
Pushed. Audit at `docs/audit-2026-05-20.md`. Three findings, with honest grades:

**1. Literature grounding — partially patched. Two gaps remain.**
- The `repair` reason tag was a band-aid against the *specific* gap the CA panel named, but the deeper question — whether a 5-tag typology is the right abstraction at all — needs the empirical corpus test in `reason-tag-empirical-test.md`. Calling it patched would be fluency.
- Boland 2022 + Goodwin 1980 are still doing rhetorical work in antiphon.md (§The deep move, §Externalize the social state) that the panel said they shouldn't. Untouched because they're foundational — if they don't say what we claimed, the *anti-mimicry* + *externalize-social-state* moves need a different paper to stand on. That's a meaningful re-read, not a docs edit.

**2. UX mistakes — 4 high-priority bugs we're shipping right now.**
- **Space-key conflict**: hold-Space silently fights page scroll, video play/pause (including in embedded Zoom previews), OS search. Picked it without thinking through the conflict surface.
- **Color-only signals** (WCAG 1.4.1): ~8% of male users can't reliably distinguish red/green bars; need glyph backups.
- **Moving-target "hold to bid" button**: lives on the seated-user card which re-sorts when roles change. Fitts violation.
- **Math-jargon "why you" line**: still reads `bid=0.62 · reason=disagree(×1.4) · quietBoost=×1.46 → 1.268` — debug output. The user's "most people can't even unmute correctly" rule violated in the most visible place.

**3. North-star drift — yes, real, significant.** Of the 8 Brennan-DM clauses in antiphon.md §Brennan-DM aesthetic, **0 are fully built and 2 are partial**. We've built fair-neutral-facilitator infrastructure (heatmap, countdown, escalation tiers) but no surface yet shows the *DM personality* — named directed asks ("Mei, your pre-read flagged X — what's your read now?"), opinionated interventions, opening frame, voice.

Three causes named:
- The adversarial panel pulled hard toward defensive design (correct legally, but timid in tone)
- Companion-website-not-tied-to-Zoom focused us on text overlays; DM-energy is fundamentally voice
- "Advisory, never unmute, verified gate" is right for v1 risk surface but mechanically timid

**Five concrete course-corrections** that re-thread the DM aesthetic without abandoning the legal/UX corrections: named directed asks on private HUD, pre-meeting position objects + drop-your-read UI, scripted opening sequence per declared mode, opinionated `Intervention` kind beyond Countdown, and one pre-recorded voice line at meeting open/close.

Want me to attack the 4 UX bugs first (fast, mechanical) or jump to the DM-personality course-corrections (slower, more interesting, addresses the actual product differentiation)?
